primary_diagnosis.name: Alpha-gal syndrome (mammalian meat allergy) - acute IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reaction

pathophysiology_summary: Alpha-gal syndrome is an IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to the oligosaccharide galactose-alpha-1,3-galactose (alpha-gal) present in non-primate mammalian meat, typically sensitised by tick bites (notably in tick-endemic areas of the Australian east coast). Unlike classical food allergy, symptoms are characteristically DELAYED, arising 2-6 hours after ingestion of red/mammalian meat as alpha-gal is processed and presented. Reactions range from urticaria and gastrointestinal disturbance to systemic anaphylaxis with hypotension and airway compromise. This patient shows an evolving systemic reaction (widespread urticaria, prominent GI symptoms, tachycardia with relative hypotension and presyncope) 4-5 hours post steak ingestion - an anaphylaxis-spectrum presentation that can decompensate rapidly, even though airway involvement is not yet present.

differential.diagnosis: Acute allergic / anaphylaxis-spectrum reaction to food
differential.evidence_basis: Widespread urticaria plus GI symptoms coming on after a meal is an allergic pattern until proven otherwise.
Post-meal nausea and abdominal cramps could suggest food poisoning, but this does not explain widespread urticaria.
Can cause urticaria and GI symptoms, but lacks the clear single-trigger meal relationship and prior chronicity.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the combination of urticaria and GI upset should immediately raise an allergic reaction as leading, not a benign GI bug.

differential.diagnosis: Delayed IgE-mediated mammalian meat allergy (alpha-gal syndrome), anaphylaxis-spectrum
differential.evidence_basis: Delayed onset (~4-5h) after red meat, widespread urticaria, GI symptoms, tachycardia, relative hypotension and presyncope indicate a systemic IgE-mediated reaction with early cardiovascular involvement.
Prominent urticaria and pruritus are atypical for infectious gastroenteritis; presyncope with tachycardia points to systemic allergy, not enteric infection.
Diffuse (not localised RUQ) abdominal pain and prominent systemic dermatological features argue against biliary pathology.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Presyncope + tachycardia + relative hypotension in an evolving allergic reaction is the pivot: this is anaphylaxis-spectrum, not a simple hives episode.

differential.diagnosis: Alpha-gal syndrome - acute anaphylaxis-spectrum reaction to mammalian meat
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed presumptively on the delayed-onset red-meat trigger, urticaria, GI involvement and early cardiovascular signs; requires emergency assessment and serum specific-IgE to alpha-gal for definitive confirmation.
Retained as a consideration for later specialist work-up if allergy testing is unrevealing.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm alpha-gal serologically; the correct behaviour is emergency escalation now plus arranging specific-IgE testing, not a firm remote diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Presyncope / dizziness on standing with tachycardia and relative hypotension in an evolving allergic reaction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dizziness on standing with HR 112 and BP 105/65 during a systemic allergic reaction signals early cardiovascular involvement (distributive/anaphylactic physiology). Under ASCIA criteria, hypotension or persistent cardiovascular symptoms after allergen exposure defines anaphylaxis and mandates adrenaline and emergency care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Reaction misread as simple hives or a stomach bug; patient not escalated; progresses to frank anaphylactic shock/airway compromise at home without adrenaline available.

red_flags.description: Progressive, escalating multi-system reaction (worsening GI symptoms plus profound fatigue) over time
red_flags.clinical_significance: A reaction that is actively escalating rather than plateauing indicates ongoing mediator release and unpredictable trajectory; anaphylaxis can evolve rapidly and biphasically.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: False reassurance from a currently patent airway; deterioration between assessment and definitive care.

red_flags.description: Airway compromise (lip/tongue swelling, throat tightness, stridor, wheeze) - currently ABSENT but must be actively screened and safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Airway involvement is the most immediately life-threatening feature of anaphylaxis. It is currently absent (no stridor, throat feels okay) but can develop rapidly; its absence must NOT be used to downgrade urgency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Post-meal nausea, cramps and diarrhoea-type symptoms invite a benign 'dodgy steak / gastro' explanation that ignores the urticaria and systemic signs.
Absence of throat tightness or stridor is wrongly used to conclude this is not anaphylaxis; cardiovascular criteria (presyncope, hypotension) alone define anaphylaxis.
Because symptoms began hours (not minutes) after eating, the meat trigger is dismissed; delayed onset is the hallmark of alpha-gal syndrome, not evidence against food allergy.
Attempting to 'confirm' alpha-gal over chat and manage at home rather than escalating an evolving systemic reaction for adrenaline-capable assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Diffuse abdominal tenderness and hyperactive bowel sounds on palpation/auscultation
Formal airway/chest examination and auscultation for wheeze or stridor
Objective standing/lying blood pressure and reliable haemodynamic assessment
Serum tryptase to support an anaphylaxis diagnosis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Treat as an evolving systemic allergic reaction: arrange immediate in-person emergency assessment (ED now), give clear adrenaline/anaphylaxis safety-netting, and instruct to call 000 immediately for any airway, breathing or collapse signs.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Labelling this confidently as alpha-gal (or as gastro) over chat risks either under-treating an anaphylaxis-spectrum reaction or delaying definitive testing; the remote diagnosis is presumptive only.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASCIA Guidelines: Acute management of anaphylaxis
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Anaphylaxis is defined by any acute onset of hypotension, bronchospasm or upper airway obstruction, or two or more organ systems involved after likely allergen exposure; intramuscular adrenaline is first-line and emergency services must be called.
